title: Chapter 8: Underworld Contact
content: The black-market information broker operated out of a tea house in Crystal City's old quarter—a neighborhood of narrow alleys, flickering neon, and the kind of silence that meant everyone was watching and no one was talking.

Ren came alone. No security detail, no assistants, no digital trail. The Salvation System had scrubbed his phone's location data and spoofed the household tracking with a looped signal that showed him in the estate library. His new enhanced senses were his only backup.

The tea house was called Tranquil Clouds. The sign was hand-painted and faded, the doorway unlit. Inside, the air was thick with the smell of aged pu'erh and sandalwood incense. Six wooden tables, half-occupied by men who didn't look up when he entered.

A woman sat at the back table. Middle-aged, gray-streaked hair pulled into a bun, face unremarkable in the way that suggested practice. She wore a simple qipao and drank tea from a cup that was older than the building.
"Mr. Zhou." She gestured to the seat across from her. "I'm Hong. Sit."
Ren sat. The tea was already poured—good oolong, which seemed to be the currency of every serious conversation in this city.
"You know who I am."

"I know you're the Liang family's son-in-law. I know you just gained access to their eastern intelligence network—I have a contact inside, don't bother looking for them. And I know you've been asking questions about unusual occurrences." Hong sipped her tea. "The question is: why?"
"Because unusual occurrences are going to get worse."

"They already have." Hong set down her cup. "In the past six months, Crystal City has seen a 340% increase in unexplained disappearances. That's not a typo. Three hundred and forty percent. The police are classifying them as 'voluntary departures' because the alternative is admitting something systemic is wrong."
"What kind of people are disappearing?"

"The kind no one look for. Migrant workers. Street vendors. Elderly people living alone. But also—" She paused. "Three array practitioners. People with bloodline sensitivity. Not Liang family, but smaller lineages. Family practitioners. Folk healers."

Ren's spine prickled. Array practitioners were rare—maybe two hundred in the entire country, most tied to old families with fading traditions. The Liang family was the apex predator in this ecosystem, but they weren't alone.
"Losing three in six months isn't coincidence."
"No. It's harvesting." Hong's voice was flat. "Someone is collecting people with array sensitivity. Taking them. And they're never seen again."
The Salvation System correlated:

`PATTERN MATCH: Previous timeline — Phase 1 Abyss infiltration`
`METHOD: Entities target individuals with dimensional sensitivity (array users, bloodline carriers)`
`PURPOSE: Threefold — (1) Eliminate potential opposition, (2) Create dimensional weak points through corrupted practitioners, (3) Use bloodline energy to accelerate portal construction`
`CURRENT STATUS: Phase 1 confirmed active`
`IMPLICATION: The Abyss has agents operating in Crystal City NOW. Not just the spore in Feng — organized human collaborators.`
"How long have you known about this?" Ren asked.

"Two years. I've been tracking it since the first practitioner vanished. A man named Qian Delun—acupuncturist, part-time herbalist, full-time array hobbyist. He could make your tea cup float. Nothing dramatic, but real." Hong's fingers tightened on her cup. "He was my friend."
"And you think the Liang family is involved?"

"I think the disappearances cluster around the Liang estate's leyline network. I think the timing coincides with seismic surveys the family conducted eighteen months ago—surveys that were supposedly for a new building foundation but covered ten times the area they'd need." She met his eyes. "And I think you, sitting in that estate, aware that something is wrong, asking questions through channels that lead to me, are either a very clever investigator or a very convenient trap."
"I'm not a trap."
"Prove it."

Ren considered his options. He couldn't tell her the truth—that he was a reborn War God fighting a dimensional invasion. He couldn't explain the Salvation System or the portal array or the spore in Feng's chest.
But he could give her something else.

"The Liang family has an array beneath their estate. A big one. It's been drawing power from the city's leyline grid for at least five years—the energy signature would show up on any practitioner's instruments as a persistent hum around 7.83 hertz, the Schumann resonance, but with harmonics that don't occur in nature." He leaned forward. "If you've been tracking anomalies, you've already detected this."
Hong went very still.

"Seven point eight-three hertz with unnatural harmonics," she repeated slowly. "You're describing exactly what Qian Delun detected six months before he disappeared. He called it 'the heartbeat.' Said it was getting stronger."
"It's going to keep getting stronger. And in approximately—" *Don't say the exact number.* "—in less than three years, that heartbeat is going to tear a hole in reality."
"You sound like you've seen it happen."

"I have." The words were out before he could stop them—a truth too heavy for casual conversation, delivered to a stranger in a tea house. But Hong didn't laugh. She didn't dismiss him. She just looked at him with the steady gaze of someone who'd spent two years believing the impossible and being told she was crazy.
"What do you need from me?" she asked.

"Information. Street-level intelligence that the Liang family's systems won't catch. Movements of people with array sensitivity. Patterns in the disappearances. And—" He hesitated. "A warning system. If the people doing the harvesting realize I'm investigating, they'll move against me. I need someone outside the household who can see them coming."
"And in return?"
"I'll find your friend. Or I'll find out what happened to him. And I'll stop the thing that's causing all of this."

Hong studied him for a long moment. Then she reached into her qipao and withdrew a small jade token—not Liang jade, older, rougher, inscribed with characters that made the Salvation System light up with recognition:

`ARTIFACT: Qian Bloodline Keepsake`
`FUNCTION: Array sensitivity amplifier (minor)`
`ORIGIN: Pre-dynastic, same era as Liang foundational artifacts`
`STATUS: Dormant, responds to proximity of dimensional anomalies`

"This was Qian Delun's," Hong said. "I took it from his apartment after he vanished. The night I went there, the door had been torn off its hinges—from the *inside*. Whatever took him, it came through dimensions, not through the door." She pushed the token across the table. "Take it. When you get close to whatever's causing this, it'll react. And maybe—maybe you'll find him."
Ren pocketed the token. It was warm against his palm, pulsing faintly with the same resonance as the Liang jade pendant and the estate's dimensional hum.
Three artifacts now. Two Liang, one Qian. Different bloodlines, different functions, same ancient origin.
*What did the pre-dynastic practitioners know that we've forgotten?*
"Thank you," he said. "I'll be in touch."
He stood to leave—
And Hong's eyes went blank.

Not sleepy. Not distracted. *Blank*, the way a television screen goes dead—the light behind it simply switching off. Her body stayed rigid, her hand still wrapped around the teacup, but the person behind her eyes was *gone*.
Then her mouth opened, and someone else spoke:
"He knows you're here, War God."
The voice wasn't Hong's. It layered over itself—male and female, old and young, human and something that had never been human. It resonated in the marrow of Ren's bones.

"The old one beyond the door. The Waiter in the Dark. He has watched you since your birth in the previous thread. He watched you die. And now—" A smile that wasn't Hong's stretched across her face. "—now he watches you *return*. How delicious."
The Salvation System:

`🚨 CRITICAL ALERT`
`ENTITY MANIFESTATION: Level 3 (Remote possession via dimensional micro-fracture)`
`ORIGIN: Abyss Main Consciousness (The Will Beyond/The Waiter in the Dark)`
`DANGER: This is NOT a spore manifestation. This is the primary entity itself, speaking through a micro-crack in reality.`
`IMMEDIATE ACTION: Break the connection. Do NOT engage in conversation.`

Ren grabbed the jade token and *pushed*—not physically, but with the one percent of War God power that now lived in his cells. A pulse of controlled energy, aimed at the micro-fracture the entity was using as a conduit.
Hong gasped. Her eyes snapped back into focus—wide, terrified, streaming tears.
"I— what— I couldn't—" She was shaking. "Something was *inside* my head."
"It's gone. You're safe." Ren gripped her wrist, checking her pulse. Rapid but stable. The possession had lasted less than ten seconds—too short to cause permanent damage, too long to ignore.
"He called you *War God*." Hong's voice was barely a whisper. "Who are you?"

Ren didn't answer. He couldn't. Because the entity had known him—known his title, his history, the fact that he'd come back from the dead. And it had spoken through a woman who'd never been infected, never been near a spore, never shown any sign of Abyss contact.
*A micro-fracture in reality.* The Abyss was already leaking through—not enough to manifest physically, but enough to touch minds. Enough to speak.
The walls between dimensions were thinner than he'd realized.
"I'm someone who's going to stop this," Ren said finally. "Stay away from the tea house for a few days. Don't go home tonight—check into a hotel under a different name. And Hong?"
She looked up.
"Whatever that thing said through you—it's afraid. It wouldn't have shown itself if it wasn't afraid. It knows I can stop it."
It was a lie. Or at least, it was an exaggeration. The entity wasn't afraid—it was curious. Amused. The way a cat is amused by a mouse that doesn't run.
But Hong needed to hear something that sounded like hope. And Ren needed her to keep operating, keep gathering intelligence, keep being his eyes on the street.
He left the tea house and walked into the neon-lit night, the jade token burning in his pocket like a coal.

Three artifacts. A cosmic entity that knew his name. A spore-infected brother-in-law. A father-in-law who was playing chess with the apocalypse. And a wife who was slowly, tentatively, beginning to see him as something more than a mistake.
The city hummed around him—traffic and neon and the ceaseless pulse of human activity. But beneath it all, at 7.83 hertz with unnatural harmonics, the dimensional array's heartbeat counted down.
One thousand eighty-seven days.
